He didn't die or even hit bottom as long as I knew him. The last time I saw him he was in his mid 20s. Looked good.

Before I was famous, long before I was a has-been, I would ride trains across Canada.
We're girls we're gamers get used to it
I would always find people on the train and befriend them and the tell them they could tell me any story, since we would never meet again.
And they would tell me stories, stories where they did not have to be hero or victim, but person.
This is one.
" I'm originally from Georgia and I never had no money, nor my kin neither, and I'd never killed nothin' in my life, hated huntin'."
"Nice boys teased me about that, the rest beat me."
" My Dad, he beat me for not hunting', I told him it was just another word for killin', and the back of his hand'd find me."
"He told me to grow up, like killin' made you grow up."
I Remember All.
" Anyways, I'd never been out of Georgia, and I'm in Asia. How's that for you. Been two places, Georgia and Danang. And I grew up, boy."
" I grew up fast."
"3rd day there,and me just 19,and we come upon a village and a bunch of 'em come yelling' at us and we open fire, but they was all innocent"
"That all came out later. They weren't innocent when we opened fire."
" They shouldn't a yelled so loud and ran at us so fast. What'd they expect?"

Anyways, back to the story of this friend of mine. I can't say you'll ever understand him, but to try you have to go way back.
Back to when he wasn't was.
His old man damned near killed him, see, and before he ever was.
His old man hit the bottle hard, then hit anything close even harder.
That'd be his wife.
And one night, the old man outdid himself, and left my friends mother for dead in a ditch, with the water filled with mud and blood.
And my friend was inside the wife, and the wife nearly died but she didn't die and she lived.
And the old man, he feared he killed his wife and fled in to the darkness, so as to be free.
The wife, she laid in bed, and waited to birth and said to all that would listen that if she birthed a boy, she would leave him.
And, so, that's how this friend of mine ended up with neither mammy nor pappy.
Now, this aint no made-up story like you read in a book or watch on a movie or a TV movie.
This story I'm unspooling right now, it's real, see. As real as the wonder of a child.
love to enjoy pleasure
As real as the blade of a knife as it slides through flesh.
As real as my knee.
So don't expect no lesson or nothing like that, just the story of a good friend of mine and what he did.
And there won't be any ending because my friend's still alive. I know a lot of you thought he wasn't but he is.
And I will not write his ending, but only God himself, who my friend believes in as much as you do not.
A man who's seen more sorrow than you've seen joy. A man who's seen more joy than you've seen joy.

It bothers me when people tack on "...and boy, are my arms tired!" after any statement of arrival. It misses the point of the original joke.
The original joke is, "I just flew in from ____ and boy, are my arms tired!" It's funny because the speaker twists the first part of the
can i see some more lunches
sentence to make it sound like he/she literally FLEW in from another city, like a bird. A lot of people these days miss this crucial part
and will say something like, for example, "I just got in from ___, and boy are my arms tired!" They're using it as a way to poke fun at
old-fashioned humor, when, in reality, they're missing the point altogether. Humor from previous eras has a reputation as being simplistic
or childish but it was far more language-based than the anecdotal/absurdist humor that prevails today (not that I have anything against this
style of humor).
It was more about wordplay, wit, verbal irony. I'm not saying this style of humor has primacy over contemporary comedy, just that it
deserves more credit.
